flashcards.Back: The Windows command used to display detailed TCP/IP configuration for all interfaces, including IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS servers.
A legacy command used on macOS and Linux to display or configure network interface parameters. it provides the IP address and subnet mask for active interfaces.
The modern Linux command (part of the iproute2 suite) used to display and manage IP addresses on network interfaces, replacing the older 'ifconfig' utility.
The modern Linux command used to display and modify the IP routing table. it is used to verify the default gateway and other static/dynamic routes.
A macOS-specific command used to retrieve detailed network configuration for a specific hardware interface, such as Wi-Fi or Ethernet.
A network utility available on all major OSs used to query DNS servers to resolve hostnames to IP addresses or vice versa.
A diagnostic tool that uses ICMP Echo Request messages to test reachability to a remote host and measure round-trip time.
The Windows implementation of traceroute. it identifies the path taken by packets to a destination by displaying each hop (router) along the way.
The macOS and Linux utility used to isolate routing problems by showing the sequence of routers a packet traverses to reach its destination.
A cross-platform command used to display the kernel's routing table in numerical format, showing the default gateway and active network routes.
A command used on Windows, macOS, and Linux to display the current ARP cache, which maps local IP addresses to their corresponding physical MAC addresses.
The IP address of the local router interface that a host uses to send traffic destined for networks outside its own local subnet.
The IP address of a server that handles name resolution requests. Hosts must have this configured to access resources using domain names (like www.google.com).
The address 127.0.0.1 (IPv4) or ::1 (IPv6) used by a host to send network traffic to itself, typically for testing the local software stack.
A parameter that must be verified to ensure the host correctly identifies which other IP addresses are local and which require the use of a default gateway.
The message sent by a remote host in response to a 'ping' request, confirming that the destination is alive and reachable.
A field in the IP header that prevents packets from looping infinitely. it is decremented by each router, and 'traceroute' exploits this to map network hops.
The unique 48-bit hardware identifier assigned to a network interface card. it is used for communication on the local Layer 2 segment.
A configuration where the host is set to 'Obtain an IP address automatically.' The host will use the DORA process to get parameters from a DHCP server.
A manual assignment of IP parameters on a host. Unlike DHCP, these settings remain constant and must be updated manually if the network changes.
A human-readable label assigned to a device on a network. utilities like 'ping' can use hostnames if a DNS server or local 'hosts' file is available.
The total time it takes for a signal to travel from a source to a destination and back again, typically measured in milliseconds by the 'ping' utility.
A condition where one or more data packets traveling across a network fail to reach their destination, often indicated by timed-out requests in 'ping'.
A verification step to ensure a network adapter is 'Up' or 'Enabled' before troubleshooting higher-level IP configuration issues.
A value in the host's routing table used to determine the preferred path when multiple routes to the same destination exist.
Checking for a Global Unicast Address (GUA) or Link-Local Address (LLA) on an interface using OS-specific commands like 'ipconfig' or 'ip addr'.
A powerful command-line tool for interrogating DNS name servers, often preferred by Linux and macOS administrators over 'nslookup'.
The size of the largest PDU that can be communicated in a single network layer transaction. it is usually 1500 bytes for standard Ethernet.
The physical hardware component that enables a computer to connect to a network. its settings are verified using commands like 'ipconfig' or 'ifconfig'.
In the context of host networking, APIs are used by applications to interact with the OS's networking stack to send and receive data.
